label: Localized face mask contact rash without infection or anaphylaxis
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized skin reaction from face mask contact.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized face mask contact skin reaction. We did not find anaphylaxis, mucosal involvement, skin infection, eye involvement, or another rash emergency today.
home: Avoid the suspected trigger if it is known.
Wash gently with mild soap and water, and avoid scrubbing the rash.
Use cool compresses, moisturizers, or coverings only as your clinician recommended.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or allergy medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or allergy medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of the lips or tongue, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening rash, skin peeling, or mouth sores.
Eye pain, vision change, eyelid swelling, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, allergy, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized face mask contact skin reaction.
No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal involvement, skin infection, severe medication reaction, eye involvement, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, skin infection, rapidly progressive rash, ocular symptoms or periorbital concern, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/allergy plan.

terms: clinician diagnosis localized face mask contact rash
discharge diagnosis localized face mask contact rash
localized face mask contact rash diagnosed

high_confidence_terms: clinician diagnosis localized face mask contact rash
localized face mask contact rash diagnosed
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports contact dermatitis framing from irritant or allergic exposures, trigger avoidance, gentle skin care, clinician-directed topical or allergy treatment, and escalation for severe allergic, infectious, ocular, or mucosal red flags. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized face mask contact skin reaction with anaphylaxis, infection, mucosal, ocular, pregnancy/pediatric, severe-rash, and specialist-directed pathways absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized face mask contact skin reaction
localized reaction only
no anaphylaxis
no infection, eye, mucosal, or severe-rash red flags
